Salk Project CO₂ Data Analysis

Adrianne Seiden

08/13/2025

Overview

Sample collection

CO₂ gas samples were collected from incubation jars and intact pots at UC Merced between October 29, 2024 and July 28, 2025. Both jars and pots were incubated in Sustainability Research and Engineering (SRE), room 450. For each sampling, pots were capped with a PVC chamber for approximately 60 minutes and jars were sealed with screw-on lids for approximately 24 hours. Headspace gas was sampled by hand (60 mL ea) and injected into evacuated 12mL Labco Exetainer vials. About 48 mL of sample was used to flush the vials, which were then overpressurized with the remaining 12mL.

Analysis

Samples were analyzed at UC Davis’s Stable Isotope Facility (SIF), using a Thermo Scientific Gas Bench interfaced to a Delta V Plus IRMS (ThermoScientific, Bremen, Germany). δ¹³CVPDB (‰) and at-% ¹³C are raw values, from SIF. CO₂ (ppm) - Blank and CO₂ Flux are calculated values.

CO₂ (ppm) - Blank

For the first batch (1 - 629), SIF lab air CO₂ concentrations are used as the blank value because no samples of SRE 450 lab air were analyzed with these samples. For the second batch, 6 samples of the air in room 450 were included, and an average of these values (within 1 standard deviation, n = 5) is used as the blank value. This may need to be revised for consistency.

CO₂ Flux

Since the jars had a known and constant amount of soil, flux was calculated as μg CO₂-C · g-soil⁻¹ · minute⁻¹. Since pots had variable amounts of soil (following destructive sampling of ~1/4 wedges every 10 weeks), and the chambers did not cover the entire pot, flux was calculated by approximating the headspace volume inside the chamber (μg CO₂-C · cm[V/A]⁻¹ · minute⁻¹). Flux calculations are based on Robertson, G. P., Blair, J. M., Groffman, P. M., Harris, D., Holland, E., Nadelhoffer, K., & Wedin, D. (1999). Soil carbon and nitrogen availability: Nitrogen mineralization, nitrification, and soil respiration potentials. In G. P. Robertson, C. S. Bledsoe, D. C. Coleman, & P. Sollins (Eds.), Standard Soil Methods for Long-Term Ecological Research. Oxford University Press. download link

Jar data

Reading data & summarizing

The following code cell loads data from an excel file (“Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx”) and generates summary statistics (mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum and count), grouped by crop, isotope and sampling week for δ¹³CVPDB (‰), at-% ¹³C, CO₂ (ppm) - Blank, and flux (μg CO₂-C · g-soil⁻¹ · minute⁻¹). It saves these statistics as a new tab, “Jars_Summary_Stats”, in the spreadsheet “Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx”.

import os
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import openpyxl

box_base = os.environ.get('BOX_BASE')
if box_base is None:
    raise ValueError("BOX_BASE environment variable is not set!")

excel_path = os.path.join(box_base, "Salk Institute Project/AKS Salk files/CO2 data/Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx")

# Read the 'Jars' tab from the Excel file
try:
    df = pd.read_excel(excel_path, sheet_name='Jars')
    print("Excel file loaded successfully!")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading Excel file: {e}")
    print(f"File exists check: {os.path.exists(excel_path)}")
    print(f"Directory exists check: {os.path.exists(os.path.dirname(excel_path))}")
    # Don't exit, just raise the error so we can see it
    raise e

# # Display the first few rows to verify data
# print("\nFirst 5 rows of the dataframe:")
# print(df.head())

# # Check column names
# print("\nColumn names:")
# print(df.columns.tolist())

# Convert isotope to integer format (removing decimals)
df['isotope'] = df['isotope'].fillna(-1)  # Replace NaN with -1 temporarily
df['isotope'] = df['isotope'].astype(int)  # Convert to integer
df.loc[df['isotope'] == -1, 'isotope'] = np.nan  # Convert -1 back to NaN if needed

# Remove rows with NaN values in key columns
columns_to_analyze = ['δ13CVPDB (‰)', 'at-% 13C', 'CO2 (ppm) - Blank', 'Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)']
df_clean = df.dropna(subset=['crop', 'isotope', 'sampling_wk'] + columns_to_analyze)

# print(f"\nRemoved {len(df) - len(df_clean)} rows with NaN values")
# print(f"Remaining rows: {len(df_clean)}")

# Generate summary statistics grouped by crop, isotope, and sampling_wk
summary_stats = df_clean.groupby(['crop', 'isotope', 'sampling_wk'])[columns_to_analyze].agg(
    ['mean', 'std', 'min', 'max', 'count']
)

# Save the summary statistics to a new tab in the existing Excel file
try:
    with pd.ExcelWriter(excel_path, engine='openpyxl', mode='a', if_sheet_exists='replace') as writer:
        summary_stats.to_excel(writer, sheet_name='Jars_Summary_Stats')
    print("\nSummary statistics saved to 'Jars_Summary_Stats' tab in the existing Excel file")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error saving to Excel file: {e}")

# Print a preview of the summary statistics
print("\nSummary Statistics Preview:")
print(summary_stats.head())
> Excel file loaded successfully!
> 
> Summary statistics saved to 'Jars_Summary_Stats' tab in the existing Excel file
> 
> Summary Statistics Preview:
>                             δ13CVPDB (‰)            ... Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)      
>                                     mean       std  ...                           max count
> crop    isotope sampling_wk                         ...                                    
> noPlant 12.0    1             -22.466808  0.485415  ...                    369.809030    16
>                 2             -19.869625  4.150075  ...                    257.756801    12
>                 3             -20.485766  2.446432  ...                    299.118337    12
>                 4             -20.051849  1.745761  ...                    180.707179    12
>                 5             -20.730754  0.828577  ...                    153.263372    12
> 
> [5 rows x 20 columns]

Plotting δ13CVPDB & CO2 flux (jars)

The following code plots δ13CVPDB (‰) vs Sampling week and Flux (μg CO2-C ⋅ g-soil-1 ⋅ minute-1) vs Sampling week as subplots on one figure. It saves the figure as a PNG file called “jars_analysis_plots_VPDB”.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use('Agg')
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
# Enable the dark_background style sheet
plt.style.use('dark_background')
# Create figure for plotting
fig=plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10));
fig.suptitle(r'Incubation Jars: $\mathrm{^{13}CO_{2}}$ Timeseries Data', fontsize=16);
# Get unique combinations of crop and isotope
crop_isotope_combinations = df_clean[['crop', 'isotope']].drop_duplicates()

# Create a color palette for consistent colors across plots
# Use a different approach with a simple list of colors
#num_combinations = len(crop_isotope_combinations)
#colors = sns.color_palette('husl', num_combinations)
colors = ['#3C57DE', '#98C65D', '#0CB2AF','#FC9D33', '#FE318E','#9F29FF']   # blue, green, teal, orange, pink, purple


# Creating Plot 1: δ13CVPDB (‰) vs sampling_week
plt.subplot(2, 1, 1);

# Plot each crop & root_isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal
    
    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df_clean[(df_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]

    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['δ13CVPDB (‰)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()
    
    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'], 
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{δ^{13}C_{VPDB}}$ (‰)');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{δ^{13}C_{VPDB}}$ (‰) vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend();

# Creating Plot 2: Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute) vs sampling_week
plt.subplot(2, 1, 2);

# Plot each crop & root_isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal
    
    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df_clean[(df_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]
    
    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_week
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()

    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'],
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot g}$-$\mathrm{soil^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot g}$-$\mathrm{soil^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$ vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.48);
plt.legend();

# Adjust layout and save figure
plt.tight_layout()
plots_folder = os.path.join(box_base, "Salk Institute Project/AKS Salk files/CO2 data/Plots/")
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'jars_analysis_plots_VPDB.png'), dpi=300)
print("\nPlots saved to 'jars_analysis_plots_VPDB.png'")

# Show the plots
plt.show()
> 
> Plots saved to 'jars_analysis_plots_VPDB.png'

Plotting atom % 13C & CO2 flux (jars)

The following code plots atom %13C vs Sampling week and Flux (μg CO2-C ⋅ g-soil-1 ⋅ minute-1) vs Sampling week as subplots on one figure. It saves the figure as a PNG file called “jars_analysis_plots_at-pct”. I wrote this after noticing that JP presents isotope data in atom % rather than δ13CVPDB (‰).

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

# Create figure for plotting
fig1=plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
fig1.suptitle(r'Incubation Jars: $\mathrm{^{13}CO_{2}}$ Timeseries Data', fontsize=16);
# Get unique combinations of crop and isotope
crop_isotope_combinations = df_clean[['crop', 'isotope']].drop_duplicates()

# Create a color palette for consistent colors across plots
# Use a different approach with a simple list of colors
#num_combinations = len(crop_isotope_combinations)
#colors = sns.color_palette('husl', num_combinations)
colors = ['#3C57DE', '#98C65D', '#0CB2AF','#FC9D33', '#FE318E','#9F29FF']   # blue, green, teal, orange, pink, purple


# Creating Plot 1: 13C-at% vs sampling_week
plt.subplot(2, 1, 1);

# Plot each crop & root_isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal
    
    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df_clean[(df_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]

    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['at-% 13C'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()

    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'], 
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{at-\% \,^{13}C}$');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{at-\% \,^{13}C}$ vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend();

# Creating Plot 2: Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute) vs sampling_week
plt.subplot(2, 1, 2);

# # Plot each crop & root_isotope combination as a separate series
# for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations.iterrows()):
#     crop = row['crop']
#     isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal
    
#     # Filter data for this combination
#     subset = df_clean[(df_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]
    
#     # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_week
#     grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()

#     # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
#     plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'],
#                  marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors[i]);

###CROP ONLY VERSION###
crops = df_clean['crop'].unique()
crop_colors = {'noPlant': '#2fb1b9', 'wheat': '#8c4dc3', 'rice': '#FC9D33', 'soy': '#FE318E'}

for crop in crops:
    subset = df_clean[df_clean['crop'] == crop]  # Remove isotope filter
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()
    color = crop_colors.get(crop, '#000000')
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'],
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f'{crop}', color=color, linewidth=2.5, markersize=8);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot g}$-$\mathrm{soil^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot g}$-$\mathrm{soil^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$ vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.48);
plt.legend();

# Adjust layout and save figure
plt.tight_layout()
plots_folder = os.path.join(box_base, "Salk Institute Project/AKS Salk files/CO2 data/Plots/")
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'jars_analysis_plots_at-pct.png'), dpi=300)
print("\nPlots saved to 'jars_analysis_plots_at-pct.png'")

# Show the plots
plt.show()
> 
> Plots saved to 'jars_analysis_plots_at-pct.png'

Cumulative flux

The following two chunks calculate weekly averages of CO₂ flux (μg CO₂-C ⋅ g-soil-1 ⋅ minute-1) for each crop, then integrate these fluxes over time using the trapezoidal rule to calculate cumulative CO₂ respiration. The trapezoidal rule integrates flux (a rate) by calculating the area under the curve between consecutive time points: \(\text{Area}_i = \frac{\text{flux}_{i-1} + \text{flux}_i}{2} \times \Delta t\). Uncertainties are propagated through the integration using the formula: \(\text{var}(\text{cumulative}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\Delta t}{2}\right)^2 (\text{sem}_{i-1}^2 + \text{sem}_i^2)\). The first chunk includes all data points, while the second chunk filters out outliers (values beyond 1 standard deviation from the mean for that week), recalculates weekly averages, and plots the resulting integrated flux over time. Both chunks print final cumulative values with uncertainty estimates and save the resulting figures as PNG files.

It is important to note that soy and rice jars have both 13C and 12C treatments, while noPlant and wheat jars have only 12C (i.e. half the sample size).

Cumulative flux no filtering

Includes all data points.

# Cumulative CO2 Flux Analysis - Grouped by Crop (Trapezoidal Rule Integration)
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Define the path to your Excel file
excel_path = "../CO2 data/Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx"

# Function to calculate cumulative flux using trapezoidal rule
def trapezoidal_integration(weeks, fluxes, sems):
    """
    Calculate cumulative integrated flux using trapezoidal rule.
    
    Args:
        weeks: array of sampling week numbers (time points)
        fluxes: array of flux values at each time point
        sems: array of standard errors at each time point
    
    Returns:
        cumulative_flux: array of integrated flux values
        cumulative_sem: array of error estimates for integrated flux
    """
    n = len(weeks)
    cumulative_flux = np.zeros(n)
    cumulative_var = np.zeros(n)  # Variance accumulator for error propagation
    
    for i in range(1, n):
        # Calculate time interval (in weeks)
        delta_t = weeks[i] - weeks[i-1]
        
        # Trapezoidal rule: Area = (y_i + y_{i+1})/2 * Δt
        trap_area = (fluxes[i-1] + fluxes[i]) / 2.0 * delta_t
        cumulative_flux[i] = cumulative_flux[i-1] + trap_area
        
        # Error propagation for trapezoidal integration
        # var(Area) = (Δt/2)^2 * (sem_{i-1}^2 + sem_i^2)
        trap_var = ((delta_t / 2.0) ** 2) * (sems[i-1] ** 2 + sems[i] ** 2)
        cumulative_var[i] = cumulative_var[i-1] + trap_var
    
    cumulative_sem = np.sqrt(cumulative_var)
    
    return cumulative_flux, cumulative_sem

# Calculate cumulative flux using trapezoidal rule for each crop
crops = df_clean['crop'].unique()
weekly_averages = {}

for crop in crops:
    crop_data = df_clean[df_clean['crop'] == crop]
    weeks = sorted(crop_data['sampling_wk'].unique())
    weekly_avg_list = []
    weekly_sem_list = []
    weekly_n_list = []
    
    for week in weeks:
        week_data = crop_data[crop_data['sampling_wk'] == week]['Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)']
        if len(week_data) > 0:
            weekly_avg = week_data.mean()
            weekly_sem = week_data.sem()
            weekly_n = len(week_data)
        else:
            weekly_avg = np.nan
            weekly_sem = np.nan
            weekly_n = 0
        weekly_avg_list.append(weekly_avg)
        weekly_sem_list.append(weekly_sem)
        weekly_n_list.append(weekly_n)
    
    # Use trapezoidal rule for integration
    cumulative_flux, cumulative_sem = trapezoidal_integration(
        np.array(weeks), 
        np.array(weekly_avg_list), 
        np.array(weekly_sem_list)
    )
    
    weekly_averages[crop] = {
        'weeks': weeks,
        'weekly_flux': weekly_avg_list,
        'weekly_sem': weekly_sem_list,
        'weekly_n': weekly_n_list,
        'cumulative_flux': cumulative_flux,
        'cumulative_sem': cumulative_sem,
        'cumulative_n': np.cumsum(weekly_n_list)
    }

# Print summary of weekly averages in order of suberin content
crop_order = ['noPlant', 'wheat', 'rice', 'soy']
print("Weekly Average CO2 Flux (no outlier filtering):")
print("Cumulative values calculated via trapezoidal rule integration\n")
for crop in crop_order:
    if crop in weekly_averages:
        print(f"{crop}:")
        for i, week in enumerate(weekly_averages[crop]['weeks']):
            print(f"  Week {week}: {weekly_averages[crop]['weekly_flux'][i]:.2f} μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n={weekly_averages[crop]['weekly_n'][i]})")

# Create cumulative flux visualization
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))

# Define colors for noPlant, wheat, rice, soy
crop_order = ['noPlant', 'wheat', 'rice', 'soy']
colors = ['#2fb1b9', '#8c4dc3', '#FC9D33', '#FE318E']  # aqua, purple, orange, pink

# Plot cumulative flux for each crop with error bars
for i, crop in enumerate(crop_order):
    if crop in weekly_averages:
        weeks = weekly_averages[crop]['weeks']
        cumulative = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_flux']
        cumulative_sem = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_sem']
        plt.errorbar(weeks, cumulative, yerr=cumulative_sem, marker='o', linestyle='-', linewidth=2.6,
                    markersize=8.5, label=f'{crop}', color=colors[i], capsize=4.5);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week', fontsize=18, weight='bold', labelpad=15);
# Create custom ylabel with different formatting - moved closer to plot
ax.text(-0.08, 0.32, 'Cumulative CO₂ Flux', transform=ax.transAxes, rotation=90, 
        fontsize=18, weight=690, ha='center', va='center')
ax.text(-0.08, 0.71, '(μg CO₂-C · g-soil⁻¹)', transform=ax.transAxes, rotation=90, 
        fontsize=16, weight=550, ha='center', va='center')
plt.tick_params(axis='both', which='major', labelsize=14);
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.48);
plt.legend(prop={'weight': 565, 'size': 16});

# Add some styling
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()

# Save the figure
plots_folder = os.path.join(box_base, "Salk Institute Project/AKS Salk files/CO2 data/Plots/")
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'jars_cumul_co2_flux_by_crop_no_filter-dark.png'), dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
print(f"\nCumulative flux plot saved to 'jars_cumul_co2_flux_by_crop_no_filter-dark.png'")

# Display final cumulative values
print("\nFinal Cumulative CO2 Flux Values (Trapezoidal Integration):")
for crop in crop_order:
    if crop in weekly_averages:
        final_cumulative = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_flux'][-1]
        final_sem = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_sem'][-1]
        n = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_n'][-1]
        print(f"{crop.capitalize()}: {final_cumulative:.2f} ± {final_sem:.2f} μg CO2-C/g-soil (n={n})")

plt.show()

>   Week 1: 265.22 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=32)
>   Week 2: 154.91 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 3: 182.91 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 4: 115.33 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 5: 93.51 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 7: 71.07 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 9: 48.12 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 11: 33.02 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=24)
>   Week 16: 18.46 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=16)
>   Week 21: 15.17 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=16)
>   Week 26: 29.00 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=8)
>   Week 31: 35.39 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=8)
> soy:
>   Week 1: 288.74 μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n=32)

Cumulative flux with outlier filtering

Filters out values beyond 1 standard deviation from the mean for that week, recalculates weekly averages, and plots the resulting cumulative flux over time.

# Cumulative CO2 Flux Analysis - Grouped by Crop (Trapezoidal Rule Integration)
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Define the path to your Excel file
excel_path = "../CO2 data/Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx"

# Function to filter outliers (values beyond 1 standard deviation)
def filter_outliers(data):
    """Remove values beyond 1 standard deviation from the mean"""
    mean = data.mean()
    std = data.std()
    return data[(data >= mean - std) & (data <= mean + std)]

# Function to calculate cumulative flux using trapezoidal rule
def trapezoidal_integration(weeks, fluxes, sems):
    """
    Calculate cumulative integrated flux using trapezoidal rule.
    
    Args:
        weeks: array of sampling week numbers (time points)
        fluxes: array of flux values at each time point
        sems: array of standard errors at each time point
    
    Returns:
        cumulative_flux: array of integrated flux values
        cumulative_sem: array of error estimates for integrated flux
    """
    n = len(weeks)
    cumulative_flux = np.zeros(n)
    cumulative_var = np.zeros(n)  # Variance accumulator for error propagation
    
    for i in range(1, n):
        # Calculate time interval (in weeks)
        delta_t = weeks[i] - weeks[i-1]
        
        # Trapezoidal rule: Area = (y_i + y_{i+1})/2 * Δt
        trap_area = (fluxes[i-1] + fluxes[i]) / 2.0 * delta_t
        cumulative_flux[i] = cumulative_flux[i-1] + trap_area
        
        # Error propagation for trapezoidal integration
        # var(Area) = (Δt/2)^2 * (sem_{i-1}^2 + sem_i^2)
        trap_var = ((delta_t / 2.0) ** 2) * (sems[i-1] ** 2 + sems[i] ** 2)
        cumulative_var[i] = cumulative_var[i-1] + trap_var
    
    cumulative_sem = np.sqrt(cumulative_var)
    
    return cumulative_flux, cumulative_sem

# Calculate cumulative flux using trapezoidal rule for each crop
crops = df_clean['crop'].unique()
weekly_averages = {}

for crop in crops:
    crop_data = df_clean[df_clean['crop'] == crop]
    weeks = sorted(crop_data['sampling_wk'].unique())
    weekly_avg_list = []
    weekly_sem_list = []
    weekly_n_list = []
    
    for week in weeks:
        week_data = crop_data[crop_data['sampling_wk'] == week]['Flux (μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute)']
        filtered_data = filter_outliers(week_data)
        n_filtered = len(filtered_data)
        if n_filtered > 0:
            weekly_avg = filtered_data.mean()
            weekly_sem = filtered_data.sem()
        else:
            weekly_avg = week_data.mean()
            weekly_sem = week_data.sem()
        weekly_avg_list.append(weekly_avg)
        weekly_sem_list.append(weekly_sem)
        weekly_n_list.append(n_filtered)
    
    # Use trapezoidal rule for integration
    cumulative_flux, cumulative_sem = trapezoidal_integration(
        np.array(weeks), 
        np.array(weekly_avg_list), 
        np.array(weekly_sem_list)
    )
    
    weekly_averages[crop] = {
        'weeks': weeks,
        'weekly_flux': weekly_avg_list,
        'weekly_sem': weekly_sem_list,
        'weekly_n': weekly_n_list,
        'cumulative_flux': cumulative_flux,
        'cumulative_sem': cumulative_sem,
        'cumulative_n': np.cumsum(weekly_n_list)
    }

# Print summary of weekly averages in order of suberin content
crop_order = ['noPlant', 'wheat', 'rice', 'soy']
print("Weekly Average CO2 Flux (after outlier filtering):")
print("Cumulative values calculated via trapezoidal rule integration\n")
for crop in crop_order:
    if crop in weekly_averages:
        print(f"{crop}:")
        for i, week in enumerate(weekly_averages[crop]['weeks']):
            avg = weekly_averages[crop]['weekly_flux'][i]
            n = weekly_averages[crop]['weekly_n'][i]
            print(f"  Week {week}: {avg:.2f} μg CO2-C/g-soil/minute (n={n})")

# Create cumulative flux visualization
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 8))

# Define colors for noPlant, wheat, rice, soy
crop_order = ['noPlant', 'wheat', 'rice', 'soy']
colors = ['#2fb1b9', '#8c4dc3', '#FC9D33', '#FE318E']  # aqua, purple, orange, pink

# Plot cumulative flux for each crop WITH error bars
for i, crop in enumerate(crop_order):
    if crop in weekly_averages:
        weeks = weekly_averages[crop]['weeks']
        cumulative = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_flux']
        cumulative_sem = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_sem']
        plt.errorbar(weeks, cumulative, yerr=cumulative_sem, marker='o', linestyle='-', linewidth=2.5,
                    markersize=8, label=f'{crop}', color=colors[i], capsize=4);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week', fontsize=12);
plt.ylabel(r'Cumulative $\mathrm{CO_2}$ Flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot g}$-$\mathrm{soil^{-1})}$', fontsize=12);
plt.title('Cumulative $\mathrm{CO_2}$ Flux by Crop (outliers removed, trapezoidal integration)', fontsize=14, pad=20);
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend(fontsize=11);

# Add some styling
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()

# Save the figure
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'jars_cumul_co2_flux_by_crop_filtered.png'), dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
print(f"\nCumulative flux plot saved to 'jars_cumul_co2_flux_by_crop_filtered.png'")

# Display final cumulative values
print("\nFinal Cumulative CO2 Flux Values (Trapezoidal Integration):")
for crop in crop_order:
    if crop in weekly_averages:
        final_cumulative = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_flux'][-1]
        final_sem = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_sem'][-1]
        n = weekly_averages[crop]['cumulative_n'][-1]
        print(f"{crop.capitalize()}: {final_cumulative:.2f} ± {final_sem:.2f} μg CO2-C/g-soil (n={n})")

plt.show()

Pot data

Reading data & summarizing

The following code cell loads data from an excel file and generates summary statistics (mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum and count), grouped by crop, isotope and sampling week for δ13CVPDB (‰), at-% 13C, CO2 (ppm), and flux (μg CO2-C ⋅ cm[V/A]-1 ⋅ minute-1). It saves these statistics as a new tab, “Pots_Summary_Stats”, in the spreadsheet “Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx”.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import openpyxl
import os
box_base = os.environ.get('BOX_BASE')
if box_base is None:
    raise ValueError("BOX_BASE environment variable is not set!")

excel_path = os.path.join(box_base, "Salk Institute Project/AKS Salk files/CO2 data/Compiled_CO2_data.xlsx")

# Read the 'Pots' tab from the Excel file
try:
    df1 = pd.read_excel(excel_path, sheet_name='Pots')
    print("Excel file loaded successfully!")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading Excel file: {e}")
    import sys
    sys.exit(1)

# # Display the first few rows to verify data
# print("\nFirst 5 rows of the dataframe:")
# print(df1.head())

# # Check column names
# print("\nColumn names:")
# print(df1.columns.tolist())

# Convert isotope to integer format (removing decimals)
df1['isotope'] = df1['isotope'].fillna(-1)  # Replace NaN with -1 temporarily
df1['isotope'] = df1['isotope'].astype(int)  # Convert to integer
df1.loc[df1['isotope'] == -1, 'isotope'] = np.nan  # Convert -1 back to NaN if needed

# Remove rows with NaN values in key columns
columns_to_analyze = ['δ13CVPDB (‰)', 'at-% 13C', 'CO2 (ppm) - Blank', 'Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute)']
df1_clean = df1.dropna(subset=['crop', 'isotope', 'sampling_wk'] + columns_to_analyze)

# print(f"\nRemoved {len(df1) - len(df1_clean)} rows with NaN values")
# print(f"Remaining rows: {len(df1_clean)}")

# Generate summary statistics grouped by crop, isotope, and sampling_wk
summary_stats1 = df1_clean.groupby(['crop', 'isotope', 'sampling_wk'])[columns_to_analyze].agg(
    ['mean', 'std', 'min', 'max', 'count']
)

# Save the summary statistics to a new tab in the existing Excel file
try:
    with pd.ExcelWriter(excel_path, engine='openpyxl', mode='a', if_sheet_exists='replace') as writer:
        summary_stats1.to_excel(writer, sheet_name='Pots_Summary_Stats')
    print("\nSummary statistics saved to 'Pots_Summary_Stats' tab in the existing Excel file")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error saving to Excel file: {e}")

# Print a preview of the summary statistics
print("\nSummary Statistics Preview:")
print(summary_stats1.head())
> Excel file loaded successfully!
> 
> Summary statistics saved to 'Pots_Summary_Stats' tab in the existing Excel file
> 
> Summary Statistics Preview:
>                             δ13CVPDB (‰)             ... Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute)      
>                                     mean        std  ...                            max count
> crop    isotope sampling_wk                          ...                                     
> noPlant 12.0    30            -11.745726   0.681702  ...                       3.924127     3
>                 40            -12.286841   0.505217  ...                       1.363547     3
>         13.0    30            -11.399520   0.022676  ...                       3.161349     2
>                 40             -8.599702   1.603134  ...                      -1.495311     3
> rice    13.0    10              8.650592  24.682512  ...                      57.600634     3
> 
> [5 rows x 20 columns]

Plotting δ13C & CO2 flux (pots)

The following code plots δ13CVPDB (‰) vs Sampling week and Flux (μg CO2-C ⋅ cm[V/A]-1 ⋅ minute-1) vs Sampling week as subplots on one figure. It saves the figure as a PNG file called “pots_analysis_plots_VPDB”.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

# Create figure for plotting
fig2=plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
fig2.suptitle(r'Intact Pots: $\mathrm{^{13}CO_{2}}$ Timeseries Data', fontsize=16);
# Get unique combinations of crop and isotope
crop_isotope_combinations1 = df1_clean[['crop', 'isotope']].drop_duplicates()

# Create a color palette for consistent colors across plots
# Use a different approach with a simple list of colors
#num_combinations = len(crop_isotope_combinations)
#colors = sns.color_palette('husl', num_combinations)
colors1 = ['#FC9D33', '#FE318E', '#9F29FF','#3C57DE','#0CB2AF', '#98C65D']   # orange, pink, purple, blue, teal, green


# Creating Plot 1: δ13CVPDB (‰) vs sampling_week
plt.subplot(2, 1, 1);

# Plot each crop & root_isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations1.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal

    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df1_clean[(df1_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df1_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]

    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['δ13CVPDB (‰)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()

    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'], 
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors1[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{δ^{13}C_{VPDB}}$ (‰)');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{δ^{13}C_{VPDB}}$ (‰) vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend();

# Creating Plot 2: Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute) vs sampling_wk
plt.subplot(2, 1, 2);

# Plot each crop & isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations1.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal

    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df1_clean[(df1_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df1_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]

    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()
    
    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'],
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors1[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot cm}$-$\mathrm{[V/A]^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot cm}$-$\mathrm{[V/A]^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$ vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend();

# Adjust layout and save figure
plt.tight_layout()
plots_folder = os.path.join(box_base, "Salk Institute Project/AKS Salk files/CO2 data/Plots/")
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'pots_analysis_plots_VPDB.png'), dpi=300)
print("\nPlots saved to 'pots_analysis_plots_VPDB.png'")

# Show the plots
plt.show()
> 
> Plots saved to 'pots_analysis_plots_VPDB.png'

Plotting atom % 13C & CO2 flux (pots)

The following code plots atom % 13C vs Sampling week and Flux (μg CO2-C ⋅ cm[V/A]-1 ⋅ minute-1) vs Sampling week as subplots on one figure. It saves the figure as a PNG file called “pots_analysis_plots_at-pct”. I wrote this after noticing that JP presents isotope data in atom % rather than δ13CVPDB (‰).

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

# Create figure for plotting
fig3=plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))
fig3.suptitle(r'Intact Pots: $\mathrm{^{13}CO_{2}}$ Timeseries Data', fontsize=16);
# Get unique combinations of crop and isotope
crop_isotope_combinations1 = df1_clean[['crop', 'isotope']].drop_duplicates()

# Create a color palette for consistent colors across plots
# Use a different approach with a simple list of colors
#num_combinations = len(crop_isotope_combinations)
#colors = sns.color_palette('husl', num_combinations)
colors1 = ['#FC9D33', '#FE318E', '#9F29FF','#3C57DE','#0CB2AF', '#98C65D']   # orange, pink, purple, blue, teal, green


# Creating Plot 1: at-% 13C vs sampling_week
plt.subplot(2, 1, 1);

# Plot each crop & root_isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations1.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal

    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df1_clean[(df1_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df1_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]

    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['at-% 13C'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()

    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'], 
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors1[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{at-\% \, 13C}$ (‰)');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{at-\% \, 13C}$ (‰) vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend();

# Creating Plot 2: Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute) vs sampling_wk
plt.subplot(2, 1, 2);

# Plot each crop & isotope combination as a separate series
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(crop_isotope_combinations1.iterrows()):
    crop = row['crop']
    isotope = int(row['isotope'])  # Convert to integer to remove decimal

    # Filter data for this combination
    subset = df1_clean[(df1_clean['crop'] == crop) & (df1_clean['isotope'] == isotope)]

    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()
    
    # Plot the mean values with predefined color from index
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'],
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f"{isotope}C {crop}", color=colors1[i]);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week');
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot cm}$-$\mathrm{[V/A]^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$');
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot cm}$-$\mathrm{[V/A]^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$ vs Sampling Week');
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend();

# Adjust layout and save figure
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'pots_analysis_plots_at-pct.png'), dpi=300)
print("\nPlots saved to 'pots_analysis_plots_at-pct.png'")

# Show the plots
plt.show()
> 
> Plots saved to 'pots_analysis_plots_at-pct.png'

Plotting pot CO2 flux by crop only

The following code plots CO2 flux (μg CO2-C ⋅ cm[V/A]-1 ⋅ minute-1) vs Sampling week grouped by crop only, ignoring isotope treatments. This provides a simplified view of how each crop performs overall. It really only changes noPlant and soy, the rest of the crops we only have 13C treatments.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

# Create figure for plotting
fig4=plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
fig4.suptitle(r'Intact Pots: $\mathrm{CO_2}$ Flux by Crop', fontsize=16);

# Get unique crops
crops = df1_clean['crop'].unique()

# Define colors for crops (matching previous plots)
crop_colors = {'noPlant': '#2fb1b9', 'wheat': '#8c4dc3', 'rice': '#FC9D33', 'soy': '#FE318E'}  # aqua, purple, orange, pink

# Plot CO2 flux for each crop (ignoring isotope)
for crop in crops:
    # Filter data for this crop (all isotopes combined)
    subset = df1_clean[df1_clean['crop'] == crop]
    
    # Calculate mean and standard error for each sampling_wk
    grouped = subset.groupby('sampling_wk')['Flux (μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute)'].agg(['mean', 'sem']).reset_index()
    
    # Use predefined color if available, otherwise use default
    color = crop_colors.get(crop, '#000000')
    
    # Plot the mean values
    plt.errorbar(grouped['sampling_wk'], grouped['mean'], yerr=grouped['sem'],
                 marker='o', linestyle='-', label=f'{crop}', color=color, linewidth=2.5, markersize=8);

plt.xlabel('Sampling Week', fontsize=12);
plt.ylabel(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot cm}$-$\mathrm{[V/A]^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$', fontsize=12);
plt.title(r'$\mathrm{CO_2}$ flux $\mathrm{(μg CO_2}$-$\mathrm{C \cdot cm}$-$\mathrm{[V/A]^{-1} \cdot minute^{-1}})$ vs Sampling Week by Crop', fontsize=14, pad=20);
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7);
plt.legend(fontsize=11);

# Add some styling
ax = plt.gca()
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)

# Adjust layout and save figure
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(plots_folder, 'pots_co2_flux_by_crop.png'), dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
print("\nPlot saved to 'pots_co2_flux_by_crop.png'")

# Show the plot
plt.show()
> 
> Plot saved to 'pots_co2_flux_by_crop.png'

> 
> Cumulative flux plot saved to 'pots_cumul_co2_flux_by_crop.png'
> 
> Final Cumulative CO2 Flux Values (Pots, Trapezoidal Integration):
> Rice: 590.21 ± 63.07 μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute (n=6)
> Soy: 224.63 ± 2.17 μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute (n=9)
> Wheat: 166.65 ± 2.40 μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute (n=8)
> Noplant: 7.01 ± 1.39 μg CO2-C/cm[V/A]/minute (n=8)

Statistical Methods in Salk_CO2.Rmd

This R Markdown document employs a range of descriptive and exploratory statistical methods to analyze CO₂ flux and isotope data. Here’s a comprehensive overview:

Data Preparation and Cleaning

The analysis begins with systematic data validation:

  • Missing data removal: Rows with NaN values in key columns (δ¹³CVPDB, at-% ¹³C, CO₂ concentration, flux) are excluded

  • Type conversion: The isotope variable is converted to integer format to maintain consistency across grouping operations

  • Validation: Data completeness is checked across crop, isotope, and sampling week dimensions

Descriptive Statistics

Summary Statistics Tables

The document generates grouped summary statistics using:

  • Mean: Central tendency for each measurement

  • Standard Deviation (std): Measure of within-group variability

  • Minimum and Maximum: Range of observed values

  • Count (n): Sample size for each group

  • Grouping dimensions: crop × isotope × sampling week

Uncertainty Quantification

Standard Error of the Mean (SEM)

Throughout all plotting sections, error bars represent the SEM:

\[\text{SEM} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]

This represents the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the mean, indicating precision of the weekly average estimates.

Trapezoidal Rule Numerical Integration

This is the primary calculation method for cumulative flux analysis:

Basic Formula

For consecutive time points, the trapezoidal approximation calculates:

\[\text{Area}_i = \frac{\text{flux}_{i-1} + \text{flux}_i}{2} \times \Delta t\]

where \(\Delta t\) is the time interval (in weeks) between consecutive measurements.

Cumulative Integration

The cumulative flux at time point \(n\) is:

\[\text{Cumulative Flux}_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Area}_i\]

Key advantage: This method respects flux as a rate measurement and properly integrates over variable or irregular sampling intervals.

Error Propagation Through Integration

Uncertainties are systematically propagated through the trapezoidal integration:

\[\text{var}(\text{Cumulative Flux}_n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\Delta t}{2}\right)^2 \times (\text{sem}_{i-1}^2 + \text{sem}_i^2)\]

The cumulative standard error is then:

\[\text{SEM}_{\text{cumulative}} = \sqrt{\text{var}(\text{Cumulative Flux}_n)}\]

This reflects how uncertainties compound as more measurements are integrated, assuming independence between weekly measurements.

Outlier Filtering Method

An optional 1-standard-deviation filter is applied in the outlier removal analysis:

For each week, values outside the range \([\bar{x} - \sigma, \bar{x} + \sigma]\) are excluded from recalculation of weekly statistics. This provides two versions of results:

  • Unfiltered: All observations included

  • Filtered: Extreme values removed to reduce influence of potential measurement errors

Data Grouping Strategies

Crop + Isotope Combinations

Used in detailed isotope plots (VPDB and at-% analyses):
- Separates ¹³C-labeled and ¹²C-labeled treatments
- Enables crop-specific and isotope-specific comparisons

Crop Only

Used in simplified flux visualizations:
- Combines all isotope treatments within each crop
- Provides aggregate flux perspective
- Accounts for different sample sizes between treatments

Summary of Analytical Approach

The analysis is primarily descriptive and exploratory rather than inferential:

Included:

✓ Temporal flux integration via trapezoidal rule

✓ Systematic uncertainty quantification (SEM, error propagation)

✓ Data quality control (outlier detection, missing data handling)

✓ Time series visualization with error bands

Not included:

✗ Hypothesis testing (no p-values, t-tests, ANOVA)

✗ Confidence intervals (beyond error bars)

✗ Comparative statistical models or regression

✗ Assumptions testing (normality, homogeneity)

The analysis focuses on characterizing temporal patterns in CO₂ flux and isotope composition across crops and treatments while transparently representing measurement uncertainty.
